Data Wrangling Project 1 Questions 4-6

TJ Cook

3/31/2026

#Cleaning

Real_World_Fuel_Efficiency <- read_csv("Real-World_Fuel_Efficiency.csv")
## Rows: 106 Columns: 14
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (6): MAKE, MODEL, Hybrid/Non-Hybrid, STANDARD TYPE, ESTIMATED FUEL COSTS...
## dbl (8): YEAR, EPA RATING (CITY), Vehicle Count, TOTAL ACTUAL MILES, TOTAL A...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
clean_numeric <- function(x) {
    as.numeric(gsub("[\\$, ]", "", x))
}

RWFE <- Real_World_Fuel_Efficiency %>%
    mutate(`TOTAL ACTUAL MILES` = clean_numeric(`TOTAL ACTUAL MILES`), `TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL` = clean_numeric(`TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL`),
        `ESTIMATED FUEL COSTS PER GALLON` = clean_numeric(`ESTIMATED FUEL COSTS PER GALLON`),
        `FUEL COSTS PER MILE` = clean_numeric(`FUEL COSTS PER MILE`))

=================

Question 4

=================

#4a

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `EPA RATING (CITY)`)) + geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "skyblue",
    color = "black") + labs(title = "Distributions of EPA City Ratings", x = "EPA Rating (City)",
    y = "Amount")

RWFE %>%
    summarize(Mean = mean(`EPA RATING (CITY)`, na.rm = TRUE), SD = sd(`EPA RATING (CITY)`,
        na.rm = TRUE), Min = min(`EPA RATING (CITY)`, na.rm = TRUE), Median = median(`EPA RATING (CITY)`,
        na.rm = TRUE), Max = max(`EPA RATING (CITY)`, na.rm = TRUE))
## # A tibble: 1 × 5
##    Mean    SD   Min Median   Max
##   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>
## 1  23.3  11.5     9     19    55
# The units are miles per gallon (MPG). The distribution shows there's a big
# peak just before the 20's, but has a pretty long tail because of hybrid
# models that have very high for their mileage numbers

#4b

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`)) + geom_bar(fill = "lightgreen", color = "black") +
    labs(title = "How Many Hybrid vs Non-Hybrid Vehicles", x = "Engine Type", y = "Count")

table(RWFE$`Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`)
## 
##     Hybrid Non-Hybrid 
##         34         72
# The dataset is dominated by Non-Hybrid cars, although Hybrids do have a good
# amount of showings in the specialized sedans.

#4c

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `ACTUAL FUEL ECONOMY Geotab`)) + geom_boxplot(fill = "orange") +
    labs(title = "Boxplot of Actual Fuel Economy", x = "Actual MPG")

summary(RWFE$`ACTUAL FUEL ECONOMY Geotab`)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##    5.35   10.54   14.93   19.88   26.09   54.58
# The data shows that there is a pretty wide spread. The median is a lot lower
# than the max, hinting that there are high efficiency outliers.

=================

Question 5

=================

#5a

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`, y = `ACTUAL FUEL ECONOMY Geotab`, fill = `Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`)) +
    geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Fuel Economy by Engine Type")

RWFE %>%
    group_by(`Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`) %>%
    summarize(Mean_MPG = mean(`ACTUAL FUEL ECONOMY Geotab`, na.rm = TRUE))
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##   `Hybrid/Non-Hybrid` Mean_MPG
##   <chr>                  <dbl>
## 1 Hybrid                  33.7
## 2 Non-Hybrid              13.4
# Hybrids pretty clearly shows there is a much higher mean and median fuel
# economy compared to the Non-Hybrid. There's a lot more variance in the
# Hybrids though to be fair.

#5b

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `TOTAL ACTUAL MILES`, y = `TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL`)) + geom_point() +
    labs(title = "Miles Driven vs Fuel Used") + geom_smooth()
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula 'y ~ x'

cor(RWFE$`TOTAL ACTUAL MILES`, RWFE$`TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL`, use = "complete.obs")
## [1] 0.7292121
# There is a pretty strong correlation between the miles increasing and the
# fuel used also increasing, surprise surprise.

=================

Question 6

=================

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `TOTAL ACTUAL MILES`, y = `TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL`, color = `Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`)) +
    geom_point() + labs(title = "Fuel vs Miles Engine Type") + geom_smooth(se = FALSE)
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula 'y ~ x'

ggplot(RWFE, aes(x = `TOTAL ACTUAL MILES`, y = `TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL`)) + geom_point() +
    labs(title = "Fuel vs Miles Facted by Engine Type") + geom_smooth() + facet_wrap(~`Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`)
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula 'y ~ x'

RWFE %>%
    group_by(`Hybrid/Non-Hybrid`) %>%
    summarize(Correlation = cor(`TOTAL ACTUAL MILES`, `TOTAL ACTUAL FUEL`, use = "complete.obs"))
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##   `Hybrid/Non-Hybrid` Correlation
##   <chr>                     <dbl>
## 1 Hybrid                    0.936
## 2 Non-Hybrid                0.736
# The slop of the line for Non-Hybrids is steeper than for Hybrids. This
# indicates that for every mile you drive, you will use more fuel in a
# Non-Hybrid.
